displayTitle: Rods vs. Cones: Aim the Fovea

beats.narration: Your eye is the instrument of a visual test. Light enters the cornea, and the lens focuses it onto the retina, your sensor.

payload.title: THE EYE
payload.sub: How the inspector's sensor gathers light and detail.

beats.narration: The retina carries two sensors. Cones cluster in a tiny central pit, the fovea. Rods cover the periphery, out toward the edges.
Cones give sharp detail and color, but need good light. Rods are superb in dim light and motion, but blurry and nearly colorblind.

payload.heading: Cones see detail; rods see dim

beats.narration: So your sharp vision is only a thumbnail-sized spot at arm's length. You can't trust the blurry edges to catch a fine crack.

payload.heading: Sharp vision is a tiny spot

beats.narration: So aim the fovea. Move your eyes directly over every area, scan slowly, and give your eyes time. That's how indications get found.

payload.sub: Point your sharp central vision over every area, deliberately.
